title: ggen Performance Definition of Done
executive_summary: Specification-driven code generation from RDF ontologies requires strict performance SLOs across eight dimensions: build time, test execution, runtime performance, memory, benchmarks, profiling, regression detection, and resource limits. This document defines what performance completeness means for ggen.

performance_dimensions.name: Build Time SLOs
performance_dimensions.description: Compilation time for workspace (all 15 crates), validation that Rust compiler performance remains fast across incremental development.
performance_dimensions.why_it_matters: Fast feedback loop is essential for Chicago TDD (RED → GREEN → REFACTOR). Slow builds break developer flow and slow CI/CD.

first_build.description: Cold build of entire workspace (15 crates)

incremental_build.description: Incremental build after single file change

if_builds_are_slow: Run: cargo clean && cargo build --workspace (measure cold build baseline)
Run: cargo build -p ggen-core --lib (measure per-crate build)
Check: cargo tree -d (look for duplicate dependencies)
Analyze: cargo build -v 2>&1 | grep Compiling | wc -l (count crate compilations)
Profile: time cargo build --release 2>&1 | tee build.log (capture full trace)
investigation_questions: Did a new dependency add bloat? (check Cargo.lock changes)
Are there circular re-exports? (cargo tree -d)
Is code generation slow? (separate ggen sync from build time)
Are macro expansion times high? (measure per-crate)

performance_dimensions.reporting_format:
[Receipt] Build Performance:
  First build: ACTUAL ms
  Incremental: ACTUAL ms
  Status: ✓ PASS or ✗ FAIL

performance_dimensions.name: Test Execution SLOs
performance_dimensions.description: Time required to run unit tests, integration tests, and full test suite. Tests must complete fast to maintain developer productivity.
performance_dimensions.why_it_matters: Chicago TDD requires rapid test feedback. If tests take >30s, developers skip running them locally, leading to CI failures and broken main.

unit_tests.description: Fast unit test pass (lib tests only, no integration)

full_test_suite.description: Complete test pass including integration tests
scope: External collaborators (SQLite, HTTP via real clients)
full_test_suite.note: May escalate to ≤120s on first compile (cold cache)

mutation_testing.note: Longer but not required on every commit (pre-merge gate)

doc_tests.description: Validates all /// Examples blocks compile and run
scope: All 14 public crates (excluding ggen-core for length)
doc_tests.escalation: May escalate to ≤180s on first compile

performance_dimensions.measurement_method: Cargo test runner with timeout wrapper
performance_dimensions.validation_command: just test-unit && just test && just test-mutation

detailed_commands.captures: Unit test count and execution time
Full test count and execution time

if_tests_are_slow: Identify slowest test: cargo test --lib -- --test-threads=1 --nocapture 2>&1 | sort -k3 -nr
Profile test: time cargo test --lib test_name -- --nocapture 2>&1
Check for mocks: grep -r 'mockall\|Mock' tests/ (London TDD forbidden)
Check for real I/O: grep -r 'TempDir\|SqlitePool\|reqwest' tests/ (should be present)
Analyze test structure: cargo test --lib -- --list (count tests per crate)
tools_to_use: cargo profiling tools (perf, flamegraph on Linux)
test categorization (unit vs. integration vs. e2e)
investigation_questions: Is there a test with unbounded loop or sleep? (grep -r 'sleep\|loop')
Are mocks slowing compilation? (grep for mockall, should not exist)
Is there real I/O that should be cached? (check test setup)
Are there tests that run serially when they could run parallel? (--test-threads=2)

performance_dimensions.reporting_format:
[Receipt] Test Execution:
  Unit tests: ACTUAL / 10s - ✓ PASS
  Full suite: ACTUAL / 30s - ✓ PASS
  Mutation: ACTUAL / 300s, score PERCENT% - ✓ PASS
  Total tests: COUNT passed
performance_dimensions.acceptance_gate: REQUIRED for all commits (unit tests pre-commit, full suite pre-merge)

performance_dimensions.name: Runtime Performance
performance_dimensions.description: Performance of ggen during actual execution: RDF processing, code generation pipeline (μ₁-μ₅), CLI scaffolding, and template rendering.
performance_dimensions.why_it_matters: ggen is a code generator. Users run it hundreds of times per day. 3-5 second overhead per run accumulates to hours of lost productivity per developer per week.

rdf_processing.description: Time to load, parse, and process RDF ontology via Oxigraph

stages: μ₄ (validate): SHACL gate checks, profile enforcement

cli_scaffolding.description: New project scaffolding: CLI startup + initialization + writing files

template_rendering.description: Tera template rendering time per output file

performance_dimensions.validation_command: ggen sync --audit true && ggen sync --dry_run true

detailed_commands.command: cargo run --release -p ggen-cli --bin ggen -- sync --audit true 2>&1 | grep duration

if_runtime_is_slow: Enable OTEL tracing: RUST_LOG=trace ggen sync --audit true 2>&1 | tee runtime.log
Identify slow stage: grep 'duration_ms' runtime.log | sort -k2 -rn
Profile slow stage: cargo flamegraph --bin ggen -- sync --audit true (Linux only)
Measure per-operation: ggen sync --audit true --profile detailed 2>&1 (if available)
Check SPARQL query performance: time ggen route list 2>&1
tools_to_use: flamegraph (Linux): cargo install flamegraph && cargo flamegraph --bin ggen
perf (Linux): perf record --bin ggen && perf report
investigation_questions: Which pipeline stage is slowest? (check OTEL spans)
Is RDF parsing the bottleneck? (Oxigraph performance)
Are SPARQL queries inefficient? (check query plans)
Is template rendering slow? (Tera performance)
Is I/O the bottleneck? (disk write performance)

pass_criteria: OTEL spans captured for all stages
performance_dimensions.reporting_format:
[Receipt] Runtime Performance:
  RDF processing: ACTUAL / 5s - ✓ PASS
  Pipeline (μ₁-μ₅): ACTUAL / 3s - ✓ PASS
  CLI init: ACTUAL / 3s - ✓ PASS
  Stages: μ₁=X μ₂=Y μ₃=Z μ₄=W μ₅=V
performance_dimensions.acceptance_gate: REQUIRED for features touching μ₁-μ₅ pipeline

performance_dimensions.name: Memory SLOs
performance_dimensions.description: Memory usage during code generation and runtime. ggen must handle large ontologies without unbounded heap growth.
performance_dimensions.why_it_matters: Memory leaks or unbounded allocations cause CI/CD to OOM on large projects. Deterministic memory usage is essential for reproducible builds.

generation_memory.description: Maximum heap usage during ggen sync execution

cli_startup_memory.description: Heap usage on ggen --help or ggen --version

no_unbounded_allocations.description: No loops that allocate without bounds
scope: Vec::push in loops must be pre-sized

performance_dimensions.measurement_method: Runtime memory profiling via /proc/self/status (Linux) or Instruments (macOS)
performance_dimensions.validation_command: cargo run --release -p ggen-cli --bin ggen -- sync --audit true && watch -n 0.1 'ps aux | grep ggen'

detailed_commands.captures: Peak RSS memory and wall-clock time

detailed_commands.command: echo 'sample proc_mem' && for i in {1..5}; do ps aux | grep ggen | grep -v grep | awk '{print $6}'; sleep 1; done
valgrind --tool=massif ggen sync --audit true 2>&1 && cat massif.out | grep 'Peak'

if_memory_is_high: Profile with Valgrind: valgrind --tool=massif ggen sync 2>&1 && massif-visualizer massif.out
Check for leaks: valgrind --leak-check=full ggen sync 2>&1 | grep -A 10 'leaked'
Analyze allocations: cargo build --release && perf record --call-graph=dwarf ggen sync && perf report
Check RDF graph size: ggen route list 2>&1 | grep 'triples' (triple count)
tools_to_use: ps aux | grep - monitor running process
investigation_questions: Is RDF graph loaded all-at-once? (should stream if possible)
Are SPARQL results cached unnecessarily? (free after use)
Is template rendering keeping all templates in memory? (render incrementally)
Are artifacts stored before writing? (stream to disk instead)

pass_criteria: No unbounded allocations in code review
performance_dimensions.reporting_format:
[Receipt] Memory Performance:
  Peak RSS: ACTUAL MB / 100 MB - ✓ PASS
  CLI startup: ACTUAL MB / 50 MB - ✓ PASS
  Valgrind: LEAKS leaks found - ✓ PASS
performance_dimensions.acceptance_gate: REQUIRED for features with large data structures or loops

performance_dimensions.name: Benchmarks
performance_dimensions.description: Criterion.rs benchmarks exist for hot paths (20% of code that uses 80% of runtime). Benchmarks are the source of truth for performance regression detection.
performance_dimensions.why_it_matters: Benchmarks prevent performance regressions. Without benchmarks, slow commits sneak into main. With benchmarks, regression detection is automated.

hot_path_coverage.description: Criterion benchmarks measure the 20% of code that uses 80% of CPU time

performance_dimensions.measurement_method: Criterion.rs benchmark runner with SLO thresholds
performance_dimensions.validation_command: just bench 2>&1 | tee bench_results.txt && grep -i 'failed\|regress' bench_results.txt

detailed_commands.expected_output: help_command: XX ms/iter, version_command: YY ms/iter
[Receipt] Transpile Performance: PASS, [Receipt] Schema Generation: PASS
At least 6 benchmark groups with HTML reports

if_benchmarks_fail: Re-run single benchmark: cargo bench --bench BENCHMARK_NAME -- --test
Check for system load: top -bn1 | head -20 (check CPU, memory)
Compare against baseline: cargo bench --bench BENCHMARK_NAME -- --compare baseline_branch
Review recent commits: git log --oneline -20 (look for recent changes)
Profile hot path: cargo flamegraph --bench BENCHMARK_NAME
tools_to_use: Criterion.rs - benchmark runner with statistical analysis
investigation_questions: Did this commit change the hot path? (check git diff)
Is system load high? (top, vmstat, iostat)
Are benchmarks run with --release? (benchmarks must use release mode)
Is there variance in timing? (re-run 3 times to check)
Is the baseline stale? (regenerate baseline after optimization)

performance_dimensions.reporting_format:
[Receipt] Benchmarks:
  cli_startup: PASS (XX ms/iter)
  schema_layer: PASS (all SLOs met)
  marketplace: PASS (YY μs/iter)
  Status: ✓ ALL PASS
performance_dimensions.acceptance_gate: REQUIRED for all commits (gated in CI)

performance_dimensions.name: Profiling & Analysis
performance_dimensions.description: Profiling data must be captured BEFORE any optimization claims are made. No optimization without evidence.
performance_dimensions.why_it_matters: Optimizations are guesses without profiling data. Profiling proves where time is actually spent. Fixes based on guesses often miss the real bottleneck.

profile_data_required.threshold: Flamegraph + perf report for any performance claim
profile_data_required.description: Proof that time is spent where claimed

flamegraph_collection.description: Visual representation of where time is spent

perf_analysis.description: Statistical sampling of CPU usage across functions
perf_analysis.command: perf record -g --call-graph=dwarf ggen sync && perf report

performance_dimensions.validation_command: cargo install flamegraph && cargo flamegraph --release --bin ggen -- sync --audit true

detailed_commands.command: perf record -F 99 -g ggen sync --audit true && perf report -n | head -30
cargo flamegraph --release --bin ggen -- sync --audit true && ls -lh target/flamegraph.svg
time ggen sync --audit true 2>&1 | tee profile_output.txt

if_profiling_data_missing: Install Linux tools: sudo apt-get install linux-tools-generic (Linux)
Generate flamegraph: cargo install flamegraph && cargo flamegraph --release --bin ggen -- sync
Generate perf report: perf record -g ggen sync && perf report
Analyze hot functions: perf report | grep '^[^ \t]' | head -20
investigation_questions: What is the top time-consuming function? (perf report --stdio)
Is time spent in library code or ggen code? (perf report with symbols)
Is there unexpected system time? (sys vs. user time in time output)
Are function calls in a hot loop? (flamegraph call tree)

performance_dimensions.acceptable_variance: N/A - profiling is observational, not comparative
pass_criteria: Flamegraph generated for any performance optimization claim
perf report identifies top 3 time-consuming functions
Profiling data attached to commit/PR message
performance_dimensions.reporting_format:
[Receipt] Profiling Data:
  Top function: FUNCTION_NAME (XX.X%)
  Second: FUNCTION_NAME (YY.Y%)
  Flamegraph: target/flamegraph.svg
  Evidence: Attached to commit
performance_dimensions.acceptance_gate: REQUIRED when claiming performance improvements (>5% faster)

performance_dimensions.name: Regression Detection
performance_dimensions.description: Benchmarks are compared against baseline. Regressions (>5% slower) block merge. Baseline is updated on successful optimization.
performance_dimensions.why_it_matters: Without regression gates, slow code sneaks into main. Regression detection catches performance regressions before they land.

regression_threshold.description: If a commit is >5% slower than baseline, it fails the gate
regression_threshold.baseline: Last successful benchmark run on main

regression_detection.description: CI runs benchmarks, compares against baseline, blocks merge if regressed

baseline_updates.description: After optimization is merged, baseline is regenerated

performance_dimensions.validation_command: cargo bench -- --baseline main 2>&1 | tee regression_report.txt && grep -i 'regress\|faster\|slower' regression_report.txt

detailed_commands.command: ls -la target/criterion/*.base && cat target/criterion/*/base/raw.json | jq '.[] | {name, mean}'

detailed_commands.note: Verify regression is from intentional change or accidental slowdown

if_regression_detected: View regression report: cat regression_report.txt | grep -A 5 'slower'
Check what changed: git diff main -- crates/
Run profiling on regressed function: cargo flamegraph --bin ggen --features unstable -- REGRESSED_OPERATION
Revert suspect commit: git revert COMMIT_HASH && cargo bench
Optimize regressed path: apply fix && cargo bench --baseline main
tools_to_use: git diff / git blame to identify changes
perf record to profile regressed path
investigation_questions: Is the regression real or statistical noise? (run 3 times)
What code changed in this commit? (git diff)
Is the regressed function in the hot path? (flamegraph)
Can we revert the regression? (git revert + bench)
Is there a simple optimization? (profile + fix)

pass_criteria: If regression exists, it is intentional and documented
performance_dimensions.reporting_format:
[Receipt] Regression Detection:
  vs. main: FASTER | SAME | SLOWER by X%
  cli_startup: 1.2% faster ✓
  schema_layer: 0.3% slower (within variance) ✓
  Status: ✓ PASS (no regressions)
performance_dimensions.acceptance_gate: REQUIRED in CI (blocks merge if >5% slower)

performance_dimensions.name: Resource Limits
performance_dimensions.description: API call budgets, network timeouts, concurrent connection limits, and OS resource constraints.
performance_dimensions.why_it_matters: Unbounded resource usage causes cascading failures. API rate limits, network timeouts, and file descriptor exhaustion are silent killers in production.

api_call_budget.threshold: ≤100 API calls per sync run (baseline: 1k-10k triples)
api_call_budget.description: Track external API calls to detect runaway loops

network_timeouts.description: Prevent infinite hangs on network failures
rules: LLM API: 60s timeout (generation may take time)

file_handles.description: Track file descriptor usage during sync

memory_pressure.threshold: No swap usage during normal operation
memory_pressure.description: Prevent OOM killer from terminating process

performance_dimensions.measurement_method: OTEL span counting, lsof monitoring, system metrics
performance_dimensions.validation_command: RUST_LOG=trace ggen sync --audit true 2>&1 | grep -E 'mcp\.tool\.call|http\.|api\.' | wc -l && lsof -p $(pgrep ggen) 2>/dev/null | wc -l

detailed_commands.command: RUST_LOG=trace ggen sync --audit true 2>&1 | grep 'mcp.tool.call' | wc -l
ps aux | grep ggen | grep -v grep | awk '{print $10}' (macOS) or awk '{print $6}' (Linux)

if_limits_exceeded: Count API calls: RUST_LOG=trace ggen sync 2>&1 | grep 'api\.' | wc -l
Identify runaway API: RUST_LOG=trace ggen sync 2>&1 | grep 'api\.' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
Check timeouts: RUST_LOG=trace ggen sync 2>&1 | grep -i 'timeout\|deadline'
Monitor connections: watch -n 0.1 'lsof -p $(pgrep ggen) | grep TCP | wc -l'
Check file handles: lsof -p $(pgrep ggen) | head -50
tools_to_use: RUST_LOG=trace - span counting for API calls
lsof - list open files by process
investigation_questions: Is there an API call in a loop? (grep for nested loops)
Are connections being closed? (check code for .drop() or close())
Is there a memory leak? (check for Vec/HashMap growth)
Are there dangling file handles? (check for error paths)
Is timeout configured? (check Client initialization)

pass_criteria: OTEL spans track all external calls
performance_dimensions.reporting_format:
[Receipt] Resource Limits:
  API calls: COUNT / 100 - ✓ PASS
  Connections: COUNT / 32 - ✓ PASS
  File handles: COUNT / 256 - ✓ PASS
  Memory: USAGE MB / 100 MB - ✓ PASS
performance_dimensions.acceptance_gate: REQUIRED for features with external calls or loops

global_constraints.acceptable_variance_policy: Global ±10% variance is acceptable across all dimensions unless otherwise specified. Variance is measured across 3 consecutive runs on the same machine.
global_constraints.first_run_penalty: First compilation after cargo clean may exceed SLOs by ±20%. Acceptable. All subsequent runs must meet SLOs.
global_constraints.variance_measurement: Run each test 3 times, take median, accept if within variance threshold
global_constraints.regression_definition: >5% slower than baseline = regression. Blocks merge until justified or reverted.
global_constraints.measurement_consistency: All measurements use the same hardware/OS for comparison. Cross-platform comparisons not valid.
global_constraints.profiling_requirement: Any performance claim >5% improvement requires supporting profiling data (flamegraph or perf report).
global_constraints.otel_validation: All external service calls (LLM, MCP, HTTP) must have OTEL spans. Missing spans = feature not working.

pre_merge: OTEL spans verified for external calls
performance_optimization: Identify bottleneck: perf report or flamegraph visualization
Verify improvement: just bench --baseline main (must show ≥5% improvement)
Attach profiling data: flamegraph.svg or perf.data to commit message

runtime_performance: [ ] OTEL spans captured for all stages

recovery: Profile test: time cargo test --lib TEST_NAME

commands_reference.quick_validation: just pre-commit (fast gate: check → lint → test-unit)
commands_reference.merge_validation: just check && just lint && just test && just slo-check
commands_reference.full_validation: just check && just lint && just test && just test-mutation && just slo-check

notes.evidence_first: All performance claims require supporting evidence: OTEL traces, profiling data, or benchmark results. No claimed improvements without proof.
notes.no_fabrication: Do NOT fabricate benchmark results, OTEL spans, or memory measurements. Always run real tools and capture real output.
notes.otel_spans_are_proof: OTEL spans prove external calls were made. Missing spans = feature not working. No exceptions.
notes.chicago_tdd: Tests use real collaborators (no mocks). Real I/O validates actual system behavior. Tests that can pass with mocks are fraudulent.
notes.determinism: ggen builds must be deterministic. Same inputs = same outputs, always. Hash verification is the proof.
notes.andon_protocol: Compiler errors or test failures = STOP THE LINE. Do not proceed until all gates pass green.
